text: The surgeon makes a transverse incision in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament, entering the preperitoneal space and creating a preperitoneal flap. Dissection continues inferiorly around the hernia sac.

A transverse incision is made in the peritoneum from the anterior superior iliac spine towards the medial umbilical ligament. The surgeon enters the preperitoneal space and creates a preperitoneal flap, continuing dissection inferiorly around the hernia sac. Robotic instruments facilitate precise dissection to free the hernia sac from adhesions.

Robotic instruments are used to precisely dissect around the hernia sac, freeing it from adhesions. The hernia sac and preperitoneal fat, previously incarcerated into the hernia defect, are completely reduced.

The surgeon reduces the hernia sac and preperitoneal fat from the hernia defect, identifying the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament. Dissection continues laterally, revealing a small indirect hernia with preperitoneal fat, which is also completely reduced.
The surgeon identifies the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament, then continues dissection laterally. A small indirect hernia is observed with preperitoneal fat inside, which is completely reduced.
The surgeon continues the dissection superiorly and inferiorly to clear the attachments of the peritoneal flap over the testicular vessels and vas deferens, using robotic instruments for precision.
The surgeon continues the dissection superiorly and inferiorly to clear the attachments of the peritoneal flap over the testicular vessels and vas deferens, using robotic instruments for precision.

A large mesh foam bar is inserted into the preperitoneal space and positioned over the terminal defects of the direct inguinal hernia. The surgeon sutures the mesh to Cooper's ligament and the abdominal wall using interrupted sutures with a robotic tool. The remaining mesh is then secured to the abdominal wall using T-seal.

A large mesh foam bar is inserted into the preperitoneal space and positioned over the terminal defects of the direct inguinal hernia. The surgeon sutures the mesh to Cooper's ligament and the abdominal wall using interrupted sutures. The remaining mesh is then secured to the abdominal wall with T-seal.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect using an absorbable two OB lock suture in a simple running fashion, incorporating the previously reduced preperitoneal fat into the closure in two layers.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect using an absorbable two OB lock suture in a simple running fashion, incorporating the previously reduced preperitoneal fat into the closure.
